narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—slightly above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, a key factor in admission decisions. The model estimated a 49.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as schools prioritize applicants predicted to succeed strongly. This prediction hinged on a few key aspects of your academic record and background, with your Year 3 school decile ranking of 4—below the average of 5.53—emerging as the single strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction. Coming from a middle-income family also played a significant role in the same direction, as the model associates such backgrounds with added challenges in bar success compared to higher-income peers. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 33.50—though under the 36.70 average—strongly pulled the prediction back toward passing, nearly offsetting those concerns. Your Year 1 decile of 5, close to the average of 5.71, and your plan to attend full-time, like over 92% of students, were neutral or less decisive factors. Overall, your profile shows promise in undergraduate performance and test-taking ability, but your later high school rankings and family income stood out as bigger hurdles relative to typical admitted students, who often have stronger deciles across the board. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your above-average GPA signaling real potential. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score further, as it already demonstrated protective value, or seek ways to strengthen your academic decile through additional coursework or recommendations. Exploring part-time options or financial aid programs tailored for middle-income students could also address those influences in future applications. Many applicants in similar situations succeed by targeting schools with holistic reviews or retaking key metrics—your path isn't closed, just needs strategic refinement. Keep leveraging your strengths, and you'll position yourself better for the next round.
